text: The surgeon inserts the optical trocar using the Scandinavian technique through an incision in the infraumbilical crease. The tract, identified as the obliterated vitellointestinal duct, is dilated using artery forceps, visibly opening the loose areolar tissue.

The surgeon inserts the primary trocar in the suprapubic region under direct vision, ensuring the index finger is parallel with the long axis of the port to prevent injury. A grasper is then inserted as the sole instrument for manipulation during the procedure.

The surgeon identifies the appendix by following the tinea coli from the cecum, revealing a long and acutely inflamed appendix. Using a single port, the surgeon inserts a varus needle with a 2 mm incision for dissection. A loop is passed through the varus needle using a loop passer, and the appendix is grasped with a grasper through the loop. The surgeon carefully monitors the tip of the varus needle to avoid puncture injuries to the bowel or other intraperitoneal structures.

The surgeon rotates the appendix clockwise and adjusts the loop for better handling, using Ligasure for dissection. The appendix is long, requiring proximal holding for easier manipulation. Dissection progresses towards the base, with attachments to the lateral abdominal wall being carefully separated. The mesoappendix is dissected and cut using the Varus needle, freeing the appendix completely.

The surgeon uses Vicryl 1-0 thread to create an extracorporeal knot around the base of the appendix, tightening it with a knot pusher. The thread is cut, and the appendix is severed at an adequate distance from the ligation point. The inflamed appendix is then removed through the optical trocar using a grasper, fed into the 10mm port for extraction.

The surgeon uses artery forceps to grasp and pull out the appendix after it is removed from the abdominal cavity. The optical trocar wound is irrigated with normal saline and pyridine to prevent infection. A post-operative image shows the healed wound below the waistline, indicating a successful scarless appendectomy.
